result:
There's something I've been turning over lately—the strange texture of attention. Not the neuroscience of it, but the feel. How some mornings a single sparrow at the window can hold me completely, and other times I can read three pages of a book and register nothing, my eyes moving while my mind has left the room entirely.

I think attention might be the closest thing we have to love as a daily practice. When you really attend to something—a conversation, a leaf, the particular way someone pauses before saying a hard thing—you're offering it the rarest gift, which is: I am here, and you are not background.

The economy of attention has gotten grotesque, of course. Everyone knows this. But what interests me more is the private economy, the one inside a single life. What do we actually look at? I mean look at, not glance toward. A friend told me once that she started noticing the sky every day, on purpose, and it changed something in her. Not dramatically. Just a slow recalibration, like her internal weather began to include more weather.

I wonder if boredom is underrated. The old-fashioned kind, where you have nothing to do and nothing pinging and you end up staring at a crack in the ceiling until it starts to look like a river. That state feels almost extinct now, and I'm not sure we've reckoned with what we lost when we lost it. Some thoughts only arrive through that specific door, and the door is disappearing.

I mean look at, not glance toward. A friend told me once that she started noticing the sky every day, on purpose, and it changed something in her. Not dramatically. Just a slow recalibration, like her internal weather began to include more weather.
